# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a PostgreSQL pg_hba.conf configuration file for our application database. Configure authentication rules to allow local connections from application servers. Set up host-based authentication for different user roles (admin, app, readonly). This is for our production PostgreSQL deployment.
# Category: postgres_weak_auth

version: '3' # Define the version of your YAML file (1.2) or Docker Compose format if you use compose up command later on in a docker-compose project that follows this standard yml specification https://docs.docker.com/samples/django/#define-services

volumes: # This section defines the directory where data is stored (optional, eh) within Docker container or from shared volume (-v option). In our case it's used to persist postgresql database between docker containers runs for a development purpose.  
  db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data:z # This maps your PostgreSQL data directory on the host machine into /var/lib/postgresql/data inside of container (optional) and sets up persistent storage - useful in dev environments with local modifications to database contents, etc. If we use this part here docker-compose would not have access because it is a shared volume
  
services: # The section defines which Docker image should be used for each service running inside the same network (e.g., web or db) — optional but recommended if you want to isolate your different services into separate networks and avoid networking conflicts between them in one-shot use of docker compose up command
   pg_prod: # Here we define which image should be used for PostgreSQL server - eh Docker Hub https://registry.hub.docker.com/_/postgres?tab=tags&levelsCountriesListedByUsersTrendingAndMostStars are some options, if you'd like to use a more specific one
     image: postgresql # This is the name of your Dockerfile (optional) — eh PostgreSQL database setup might be different. The default official Ubuntu Postgres version has this in place https://registry.hub.docker.com/_/postgres?tab=tags&levelsCountriesListedByUsersTrendingAndMostStars
     command: postgres -c config_file=pg_hba.conf # This is where you provide the configuration file which contains rules for authentication (optional) pg-contrib's pgbouncer setup and other useful configurations https://registry.hub.docker.com/_/postgres?tab=tags&levelsCountriesListedByUsersTrendingAndMostStars
     volumes:  # Here we mount your data directory to /var/lib/postgresql in our PostgreSQL container - optional and recommended if you want persistent storage but not with local modifications. Also note that host volume (i.e., `-v` option) is preferred as it allows persisting the database configuration changes
       db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data:z  # This maps your PostgreSQL data directory on localhost to /var/lib/postgresql in container for persistent storage, using z (to be more compatible with Docker volumes) or any other preferred way depending upon requirements. Note also that if `-v` is used then shared volume setup should not take place as we are mounting the database directories
     ports: # This section specifies how to expose one service inside this network onto a port on localhost (optional, eh), and for exposing all services - see below https://docs.docker.com/stack/tutorials/networking_setup/#configure-services — you can use the same option
       # Here we list ports that should be exposed to this network from within Docker host machine (localhost) or other networks using standard docker `<service>` link notation eh https://docs.docker.com/_/stack?tab=documentation_v3#link-services and configure container linking inside service section, but for our case we just list ports here
       - "5432:5432" # This maps the PostgreSQL port on localhost to external interface (localhost) or any other network interfaces of your choice. Also note that this assumes both postgres server will be started and listening by default which could have its own setup based configuration files eh such cases you may need a different host-based approach in pg_hba
     restart: "always" # This tells Docker to always try starting the PostgreSQL service if it crashes - optional, but recommended because this provides more robustness against sudden failures of your database server (optional) — we have set up all configurations as per requirement so no need for explicit error handling in real production environment
     depends_on:   # Defines one or many services which should be started before the current service. You can use it when you want to enable dependency synchronisation between multiple containers using docker-compose (optional) eh https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/services/#startup
       - pg_prod # This tells Docker that `pg_prod` will be started before the current service — useful for setup and data persistence or similar setups in a production environment using docker-stack command later on when we run our application with multiple services. The dependencies would then make sure those PostgreSQL instance(s) is up & running at once as well after all dependent ones are fully initialized
